/Users/mike/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/agentrail
Workflow CLI for keeping AI agents on track

Usage: agentrail [OPTIONS] <COMMAND>

Commands:
  init            Initialize a new saga
  setup           Bootstrap a project: create saga, CLAUDE.md, and register domain
  add             Add a step to the saga (for maintenance mode or ad-hoc tasks)
  status          Show current saga state
  next            Output current step prompt and context for a fresh agent session
  begin           Mark the current step as in-progress
  complete        Complete current step, optionally define next step
  plan            View or update the saga plan
  history         Show all step summaries
  distill         Distill trajectories into a skill document
  run-loop        Auto-execute deterministic steps, pause at agent steps
  abort           Mark current step as blocked
  archive         Archive current saga to .agentrail-archive/ and clear .agentrail/
  audit           Compare git history with saga history, report gaps
  snapshot        Save a point-in-time copy of .agentrail/ into the git object store
  gen-agents-doc  Write AGENTS.example.md — an agent instructions template for agentrail
  help            Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Options:
      --saga <SAGA>  Path to the project directory (default: current directory) [default: .]
  -h, --help         Print help
  -V, --version      Print version

1	# Development Process
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	This document describes the development workflow for the needs-attention project. It incorporates lessons learned and establishes patterns for future development.
6	
7	**CRITICAL**: This project follows **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** with a strict **Red/Green/Refactor** cycle and mandatory **pre-commit quality gates**. All code changes must pass the complete pre-commit process before being committed.
8	
9	## Core Principles
10	
11	### 1. Test-Driven Development (TDD)
12	
13	**Red/Green/Refactor Cycle**:
14	
15	```
16	RED: Write failing test -> GREEN: Make it pass -> REFACTOR: Improve code -> Repeat
17	```
18	
19	**Process**:
20	1. **RED**: Write a failing test that defines desired behavior
21	   - Test should fail for the right reason
22	   - Compile error or assertion failure
23	2. **GREEN**: Write minimal code to make the test pass
24	   - Don't worry about perfection yet
25	   - Just make it work
26	3. **REFACTOR**: Improve the code while keeping tests green
27	   - Remove duplication
28	   - Improve names
29	   - Simplify logic
30	4. **REPEAT**: Continue cycle for next piece of functionality
31	
32	**Benefits**:
33	- Tests document expected behavior
34	- High confidence in changes
35	- Catches regressions immediately
36	- Forces good design (testable code)
37	
38	### 2. Pre-Commit Quality Gates
39	
40	**MANDATORY BEFORE EVERY COMMIT**: No exceptions, no deferrals, no disabling checks.
41	
42	All changes must pass the complete pre-commit process. If any step fails, fix it before proceeding.
43	
44	## Development Cycle
45	
46	### 1. Feature Development
47	
48	```
49	Plan -> RED (Test) -> GREEN (Code) -> REFACTOR -> Review -> Pre-Commit -> Commit -> Push
50	```
51	
52	**Planning**:
53	- Review PRD (docs/prd.md) for requirements
54	- Check design doc (docs/design.md) for patterns
55	- Update plan.md with task breakdown
56	- Create mental model before coding
57	- **Write test scenarios first** (TDD planning)
58	
59	**RED - Write Failing Test**:
60	- Write test that defines expected behavior
61	- Run test to confirm it fails
62	- Verify failure is for the right reason
63	
64	**GREEN - Implement Minimum Code**:
65	- Write simplest code to pass the test
66	- Run test to confirm it passes
67	- Don't optimize yet
68	
69	**REFACTOR - Improve Code**:
70	- Keep tests passing while improving
71	- Remove duplication
72	- Improve naming and structure
73	- Keep functions under 50 lines
74	- Keep files under 500 lines
75	
76	**Testing** (Continuous):
77	- Write unit tests alongside code (TDD)
78	- Add integration tests for database operations
79	- Use Playwright for UI testing
80	- Run tests frequently during development
81	- All tests must pass before proceeding
82	
83	**Review**:
84	- Self-review changes before committing
85	- Check against docs/learnings.md for common mistakes
86	- Verify all acceptance criteria met
87	- Ensure all tests pass
88	
89	**Pre-Commit** (MANDATORY):
90	- Follow pre-commit quality process (below)
91	- All steps must pass - NO EXCEPTIONS
92	- Fix issues, never disable checks
93	- Update docs/learnings.md if issues found
94	
95	**Commit**:
96	- Write clear, detailed commit messages
97	- Include co-authorship attribution
98	- Reference related issues/PRs
99	
100	**Push**:
101	- Push immediately after commit
102	- Enables testing on other systems
103	- Provides incremental backup
104	
105	### 2. Pre-Commit Quality Process
106	
107	**CRITICAL**: This process is MANDATORY before every commit. No exceptions, no deferrals, no disabling checks.
108	
109	**When to Run**:
110	- Before every commit (no exceptions)
111	- After completing a feature
112	- Before switching contexts
113	- End of development session
114	- When explicitly requested
115	
116	**Pre-Commit Sequence** (Must be completed in order, all must pass):
117	
118	#### Step 1: Run Tests
119	```bash
120	cargo test
121	```
122	
123	**Requirements**:
124	- ALL tests must pass
125	- NO test failures allowed
126	- NO disabled tests allowed
127	- Fix all failing tests before proceeding
128	
129	**If tests fail**:
130	- Debug and fix the failing test
131	- Do NOT disable the test
132	- Do NOT skip the test
133	- Do NOT defer the fix
134	
135	#### Step 2: Fix Linting (No Warnings Allowed)
136	```bash
137	cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings
138	```
139	
140	**Requirements**:
141	- ZERO clippy warnings
142	- ALL warnings must be fixed
143	- NEVER use #[allow(...)] to suppress warnings
144	- NEVER use --allow-warnings flag
145	- Apply clippy's suggested fixes
146	
147	**If clippy fails**:
148	- Fix each warning properly
149	- Do NOT disable clippy checks
150	- Do NOT use allow attributes
151	- Do NOT defer fixes
152	- Re-run until completely clean
153	
154	#### Step 3: Format Code
155	```bash
156	cargo fmt --all
157	```
158	
159	**Requirements**:
160	- ALL code must be formatted
161	- Confirm no formatting changes remain
162	- Run `cargo fmt --check` to verify
163	
164	#### Step 4: Validate Markdown (if docs changed)
165	```bash
166	markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
167	```
168	
169	**Requirements**:
170	- All markdown must be ASCII-only
171	- Fix tree symbols and non-ASCII characters
172	- Use `--fix` for auto-fixable issues
173	- Manual fixes for emojis and other unicode
174	
175	#### Step 5: Validate .gitignore
176	```bash
177	git status
178	```
179	
180	**Requirements**:
181	- No build artifacts in staging
182	- No temporary files in staging
183	- Update .gitignore if needed
184	- Verify only intentional files staged
185	
186	#### Step 6: Run Software Wrighter Checklist
187	
188	```bash
189	sw-checklist
190	```
191	
192	**Requirements**:
193	- ALL checklist items must pass
194	- Address any non-compliant project elements
195	- Run with --help for project-specific requirements
196	
197	**If checklist fails**:
198	- Fix each failed requirement
199	- Re-run until all checks pass
200	- Update project structure as needed
201	
202	**AI Agent Notes**:
203	- This ensures project meets Software Wrighter standards
204	- Use `sw-checklist --help` for detailed guidance
205	- Common checks: documentation, structure, licensing, quality
206	
207	#### Step 7: Update Documentation
208	
209	**CRITICAL**: If any of the previous steps required changes, update docs/learnings.md
210	
211	**Update docs/learnings.md if**:
212	- Clippy warnings were found (document the pattern)
213	- Tests failed (document root cause)
214	- Bug was fixed (document prevention strategy)
215	
216	**Root Cause Analysis Required**:
217	When updating learnings.md for bugs or test failures:
218	1. **What went wrong?** - Describe the issue
219	2. **Why wasn't it caught sooner?** - Identify process gap
220	3. **What process change prevents this?** - Document prevention
221	4. **Add to proactive checklist** - Update process.md if needed
222	
223	**Also Update**:
224	- README.md if features added or changed
225	- CLAUDE.md if development patterns changed
226	- docs/status.md with progress
227	- docs/architecture.md if system design changed
228	- docs/design.md if design decisions made
229	
230	#### Step 8: Final Review
231	
232	**Self-Review Checklist**:
233	- [ ] All tests pass
234	- [ ] Zero clippy warnings
235	- [ ] Code formatted
236	- [ ] Markdown validated (if applicable)
237	- [ ] .gitignore appropriate
238	- [ ] sw-checklist passes
239	- [ ] Documentation updated
240	- [ ] docs/learnings.md updated if issues found
241	- [ ] No commented-out code
242	- [ ] No debug print statements
243	- [ ] Commit message clear and detailed
244	
245	#### Step 9: Commit and Push
246	```bash
247	git add -A
248	git commit -m "Clear, descriptive message
249	
250	Detailed explanation of changes...
251	
252	If bugs were fixed:
253	- Root cause: <why the bug occurred>
254	- Prevention: <what process change prevents this>
255	- Updated learnings.md with <specific section>
256	
257	[AI] Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
258	
259	Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>"
260	
261	git push
262	```
263	
264	**Commit Message Requirements**:
265	- Clear, descriptive summary (50 chars max)
266	- Detailed explanation of what and why
267	- Root cause analysis if bug fix
268	- Reference to learnings.md updates if applicable
269	- Co-authorship attribution
270	
271	### 3. Quality Standards
272	
273	**Code Quality**:
274	- Zero clippy warnings (enforced with `-D warnings`)
275	- All code formatted with `cargo fmt`
276	- Rust 2024 edition idioms
277	- Inline format arguments: `format!("{name}")` not `format!("{}", name)`
278	- Inner doc comments for modules: `//!` not `///` + empty line
279	
280	**Test Coverage**:
281	- Unit tests for pure logic
282	- Integration tests for database operations
283	- UI tests for critical user flows
284	- Edge case handling
285	
286	**Documentation**:
287	- Public APIs documented with `///` comments
288	- Module-level docs with `//!` comments
289	- README kept up-to-date
290	- Examples in doc comments where helpful
291	
292	**Tech Debt Limits**:
293	- Maximum 3 TODO comments per file
294	- Files under 500 lines (prefer 200-300)
295	- Functions under 50 lines (prefer 10-30)
296	- Address TODOs within 2 development sessions
297	- Never commit FIXMEs (fix immediately)
298	
299	## Build Process
300	
301	### Local Development
302	
303	**Initial Setup**:
304	```bash
305	# Install Rust (if needed)
306	curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
307	
308	# Install wasm-pack
309	cargo install wasm-pack
310	
311	# Verify prerequisites
312	./scripts/check-setup.sh
313	```
314	
315	**Build**:
316	```bash
317	./scripts/build-all.sh
318	```
319	
320	This script:
321	1. Builds Rust CLI (release mode)
322	2. Generates build-info.json
323	3. Builds WASM UI with wasm-pack
324	
325	**Development Iteration**:
326	```bash
327	# Terminal 1: Run web server
328	./scripts/run-web.sh 2222
329	
330	# Terminal 2: Make changes, then rebuild
331	./scripts/build-all.sh
332	
333	# Refresh browser to see changes
334	```
335	
336	**Testing**:
337	```bash
338	# Run all tests
339	cargo test
340	
341	# Run specific test
342	cargo test test_name
343	
344	# Run tests with output
345	cargo test -- --nocapture
346	```
347	
348	### Continuous Integration
349	
350	(Not yet implemented, but planned)
351	
352	**GitHub Actions Workflow**:
353	```yaml
354	name: CI
355	on: [push, pull_request]
356	jobs:
357	  test:
358	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
359	    steps:
360	      - uses: actions/checkout@v2
361	      - uses: actions-rs/toolchain@v1
362	      - run: cargo test
363	      - run: cargo clippy -- -D warnings
364	      - run: cargo fmt -- --check
365	```
366	
367	## Testing Strategy
368	
369	### Unit Tests
370	
371	**Location**: Same file as code, in `#[cfg(test)]` module
372	
373	**Example**:
374	```rust
375	#[cfg(test)]
376	mod tests {
377	    use super::*;
378	
379	    #[test]
380	    fn test_detect_rust_project() {
381	        let temp = tempfile::tempdir().unwrap();
382	        let cargo_toml = temp.path().join("Cargo.toml");
383	        fs::write(&cargo_toml, "[package]").unwrap();
384	
385	        assert_eq!(detect_project_type(temp.path()), ProjectType::Rust);
386	    }
387	}
388	```
389	
390	**What to Test**:
391	- Pure functions (no I/O)
392	- Edge cases (empty input, max values)
393	- Error conditions
394	
395	### Integration Tests
396	
397	**Location**: `src/lib.rs` in test module (access to internal API)
398	
399	**Example**:
400	```rust
401	#[test]
402	fn test_project_crud() {
403	    let temp = tempfile::tempdir().unwrap();
404	    let db = Database::open_or_create_at(temp.path()).unwrap();
405	
406	    let project = Project {
407	        name: "test-project".to_string(),
408	        // ...
409	    };
410	
411	    let id = db.upsert_project(&project).unwrap();
412	    let retrieved = db.get_project(id).unwrap().unwrap();
413	    assert_eq!(retrieved.name, "test-project");
414	}
415	```
416	
417	**What to Test**:
418	- Database operations
419	- Scanner integration (find + analyze + insert)
420	- API endpoints (future: with test harness)
421	
422	### UI Tests
423	
424	**Tool**: Playwright via MCP server
425	
426	**Setup**:
427	```bash
428	# Install Playwright MCP
429	claude mcp add playwright -s user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp
430	
431	# Verify
432	claude mcp list
433	```
434	
435	**Example**:
436	```rust
437	// In Claude Code session:
438	// "Use Playwright to test the project detail view"
439	```
440	
441	**What to Test**:
442	- Page loads correctly
443	- Table sorting works
444	- Search filtering works
445	- Detail view navigation
446	- Status update persistence
447	
448	### Test Data Management
449	
450	**Temporary Directories**:
451	```rust
452	use tempfile::tempdir;
453	
454	let temp = tempdir()?;
455	// Create test data in temp.path()
456	// Automatic cleanup when temp drops
457	```
458	
459	**Test Databases**:
460	```rust
461	let db = Database::open_or_create_at(temp.path())?;
462	// Fresh database for each test
463	// No shared state between tests
464	```
465	
466	**Mock Data**:
467	```rust
468	fn create_test_project() -> Project {
469	    Project {
470	        id: 0,
471	        name: "test-project".to_string(),
472	        path: "/tmp/test".into(),
473	        // ...
474	    }
475	}
476	```
477	
478	## Git Workflow
479	
480	### Branch Strategy
481	
482	**Current**: Direct commits to `main`
483	- Single developer
484	- Small changes
485	- Fast iteration
486	
487	**Future** (Multi-developer):
488	- Feature branches: `feature/add-export`
489	- Bugfix branches: `fix/scanner-crash`
490	- PR-based review process
491	
492	### Commit Messages
493	
494	**Format**:
495	```
496	type: Short summary (50 chars max)
497	
498	Detailed explanation of what changed and why.
499	Include context, rationale, and trade-offs.
500	
501	Bullet points for multiple changes:
502	- Added feature X
503	- Fixed bug Y
504	- Refactored Z
505	
506	[AI] Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
507	
508	Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
509	```
510	
511	**Types**:
512	- `feat:` New feature
513	- `fix:` Bug fix
514	- `docs:` Documentation only
515	- `style:` Formatting, no code change
516	- `refactor:` Code restructuring
517	- `test:` Adding tests
518	- `chore:` Build process, dependencies
519	
520	**Examples**:
521	```
522	feat: Add row numbers to project table
523	
524	Added index column with small font for easy reference in
525	discussions. Updated CSS for compact styling.
526	
527	fix: Correct refresh mechanism to capture DOM state
528	
529	Changed refresh to re-check git status without full rescan.
530	Prevents stale data when user modifies projects externally.
531	
532	docs: Add screenshot and fix markdown formatting
533	
534	Replaced Unicode tree symbols with ASCII for portability.
535	Added full-page screenshot to README for visual context.
536	```
537	
538	### Push Policy
539	
540	**Always push immediately after commit**:
541	- Enables testing on other machines
542	- Provides backup of work
543	- Makes work visible for collaboration
544	
545	**Never**:
546	- Force push to main (unless recovery needed)
547	- Rewrite history of pushed commits
548	- Skip pre-commit checks
549	
550	## Code Review Guidelines
551	
552	### Self-Review Checklist
553	
554	Before committing, review your own changes:
555	
556	- [ ] All tests pass
557	- [ ] No clippy warnings
558	- [ ] Code formatted
559	- [ ] Documentation updated
560	- [ ] No hardcoded values (use constants)
561	- [ ] Error handling appropriate
562	- [ ] No commented-out code
563	- [ ] No debug print statements
564	- [ ] Commit message clear and detailed
565	
566	### Common Issues to Watch For
567	
568	From learnings.md:
569	
570	1. **Doc comments**: Use `//!` for modules, `///` for items, no empty lines
571	2. **Unused imports**: Remove after refactoring
572	3. **Format arguments**: Use inline syntax `"{name}"` not `"{}", name`
573	4. **Needless borrows**: Trust clippy on generic args
574	5. **File size**: Keep under 500 lines
575	6. **TODO count**: Max 3 per file
576	
577	## Deployment Process
578	
579	### Pre-Release Checklist
580	
581	- [ ] All tests passing
582	- [ ] Zero clippy warnings
583	- [ ] Code formatted
584	- [ ] README up-to-date with screenshots
585	- [ ] CHANGELOG.md updated
586	- [ ] Version bumped in Cargo.toml
587	- [ ] Git tag created: `v0.1.0`
588	- [ ] Build artifacts generated
589	
590	### Release Steps
591	
592	```bash
593	# 1. Update version
594	# Edit Cargo.toml: version = "0.1.0"
595	
596	# 2. Update CHANGELOG
597	# Document all changes since last release
598	
599	# 3. Commit version bump
600	git add Cargo.toml CHANGELOG.md
601	git commit -m "chore: Bump version to 0.1.0"
602	
603	# 4. Create tag
604	git tag -a v0.1.0 -m "Release v0.1.0"
605	
606	# 5. Push
607	git push && git push --tags
608	
609	# 6. Build release artifacts
610	./scripts/build-all.sh
611	tar -czf needs-attention-v0.1.0-macos.tar.gz \
612	    target/release/needs-attention \
613	    static/ \
614	    wasm-ui/pkg/
615	
616	# 7. Create GitHub release
617	# Upload tar.gz, add release notes from CHANGELOG
618	```
619	
620	## Maintenance
621	
622	### Dependency Updates
623	
624	**Monthly Check**:
625	```bash
626	cargo update
627	cargo test
628	cargo clippy
629	# If all pass, commit Cargo.lock update
630	```
631	
632	**Breaking Changes**:
633	- Review changelog for dependencies
634	- Test thoroughly after major version bumps
635	- Update code if APIs changed
636	
637	### Database Migrations
638	
639	**Current**: Ignored ALTER TABLE statements
640	
641	**Future**: Proper migration system
642	```bash
643	# Track version in database
644	# Run migrations on startup
645	# Provide rollback mechanism
646	```
647	
648	## Tooling
649	
650	### Required Tools
651	
652	- Rust (latest stable)
653	- wasm-pack
654	- git
655	
656	### Recommended Tools
657	
658	- markdown-checker (for docs)
659	- Playwright (for UI testing)
660	- cargo-watch (for auto-rebuild)
661	
662	### Optional Tools
663	
664	- cargo-bloat (analyze binary size)
665	- cargo-audit (security vulnerabilities)
666	- cargo-outdated (dependency updates)
667	
668	## Emergency Procedures
669	
670	### Rollback
671	
672	```bash
673	# Undo last commit (not pushed)
674	git reset --soft HEAD~1
675	
676	# Undo last commit (pushed)
677	# Create revert commit instead
678	git revert HEAD
679	git push
680	```
681	
682	### Database Corruption
683	
684	```bash
685	# Restore from backup
686	cp needs_attention.db.backup needs_attention.db
687	
688	# Or recreate
689	rm needs_attention.db
690	./scripts/gather.sh /path/to/projects
691	```
692	
693	### Build Failures
694	
695	```bash
696	# Clean build artifacts
697	cargo clean
698	rm -rf wasm-ui/pkg
699	./scripts/build-all.sh
700	
701	# Reset to known-good commit
702	git log --oneline
703	git checkout <commit-sha>
704	```
705	
706	## Continuous Improvement
707	
708	### Learning Integration
709	
710	1. Document issues in learnings.md
711	2. Update process.md with prevention strategies
712	3. Add to checkpoint checklist if recurring
713	4. Consider automation for frequent issues
714	
715	### Metrics Tracking
716	
717	(Future implementation)
718	
719	**Track**:
720	- Build times
721	- Test execution time
722	- Code coverage
723	- Clippy warnings over time
724	- Database size growth
725	
726	**Tools**:
727	- cargo bench for performance
728	- tarpaulin for coverage
729	- GitHub Actions for CI metrics
730

file.content:
# Development Process

## Overview

This document describes the development workflow for the needs-attention project. It incorporates lessons learned and establishes patterns for future development.

**CRITICAL**: This project follows **Test-Driven Development (TDD)** with a strict **Red/Green/Refactor** cycle and mandatory **pre-commit quality gates**. All code changes must pass the complete pre-commit process before being committed.

## Core Principles

### 1. Test-Driven Development (TDD)

**Red/Green/Refactor Cycle**:

```
RED: Write failing test -> GREEN: Make it pass -> REFACTOR: Improve code -> Repeat
```

**Process**:
1. **RED**: Write a failing test that defines desired behavior
   - Test should fail for the right reason
   - Compile error or assertion failure
2. **GREEN**: Write minimal code to make the test pass
   - Don't worry about perfection yet
   - Just make it work
3. **REFACTOR**: Improve the code while keeping tests green
   - Remove duplication
   - Improve names
   - Simplify logic
4. **REPEAT**: Continue cycle for next piece of functionality

**Benefits**:
- Tests document expected behavior
- High confidence in changes
- Catches regressions immediately
- Forces good design (testable code)

### 2. Pre-Commit Quality Gates

**MANDATORY BEFORE EVERY COMMIT**: No exceptions, no deferrals, no disabling checks.

All changes must pass the complete pre-commit process. If any step fails, fix it before proceeding.

## Development Cycle

### 1. Feature Development

```
Plan -> RED (Test) -> GREEN (Code) -> REFACTOR -> Review -> Pre-Commit -> Commit -> Push
```

**Planning**:
- Review PRD (docs/prd.md) for requirements
- Check design doc (docs/design.md) for patterns
- Update plan.md with task breakdown
- Create mental model before coding
- **Write test scenarios first** (TDD planning)

**RED - Write Failing Test**:
- Write test that defines expected behavior
- Run test to confirm it fails
- Verify failure is for the right reason

**GREEN - Implement Minimum Code**:
- Write simplest code to pass the test
- Run test to confirm it passes
- Don't optimize yet

**REFACTOR - Improve Code**:
- Keep tests passing while improving
- Remove duplication
- Improve naming and structure
- Keep functions under 50 lines
- Keep files under 500 lines

**Testing** (Continuous):
- Write unit tests alongside code (TDD)
- Add integration tests for database operations
- Use Playwright for UI testing
- Run tests frequently during development
- All tests must pass before proceeding

**Review**:
- Self-review changes before committing
- Check against docs/learnings.md for common mistakes
- Verify all acceptance criteria met
- Ensure all tests pass

**Pre-Commit** (MANDATORY):
- Follow pre-commit quality process (below)
- All steps must pass - NO EXCEPTIONS
- Fix issues, never disable checks
- Update docs/learnings.md if issues found

**Commit**:
- Write clear, detailed commit messages
- Include co-authorship attribution
- Reference related issues/PRs

**Push**:
- Push immediately after commit
- Enables testing on other systems
- Provides incremental backup

### 2. Pre-Commit Quality Process

**CRITICAL**: This process is MANDATORY before every commit. No exceptions, no deferrals, no disabling checks.

**When to Run**:
- Before every commit (no exceptions)
- After completing a feature
- Before switching contexts
- End of development session
- When explicitly requested

**Pre-Commit Sequence** (Must be completed in order, all must pass):

#### Step 1: Run Tests
```bash
cargo test
```

**Requirements**:
- ALL tests must pass
- NO test failures allowed
- NO disabled tests allowed
- Fix all failing tests before proceeding

**If tests fail**:
- Debug and fix the failing test
- Do NOT disable the test
- Do NOT skip the test
- Do NOT defer the fix

#### Step 2: Fix Linting (No Warnings Allowed)
```bash
cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings
```

**Requirements**:
- ZERO clippy warnings
- ALL warnings must be fixed
- NEVER use #[allow(...)] to suppress warnings
- NEVER use --allow-warnings flag
- Apply clippy's suggested fixes

**If clippy fails**:
- Fix each warning properly
- Do NOT disable clippy checks
- Do NOT use allow attributes
- Do NOT defer fixes
- Re-run until completely clean

#### Step 3: Format Code
```bash
cargo fmt --all
```

**Requirements**:
- ALL code must be formatted
- Confirm no formatting changes remain
- Run `cargo fmt --check` to verify

#### Step 4: Validate Markdown (if docs changed)
```bash
markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
```

**Requirements**:
- All markdown must be ASCII-only
- Fix tree symbols and non-ASCII characters
- Use `--fix` for auto-fixable issues
- Manual fixes for emojis and other unicode

#### Step 5: Validate .gitignore
```bash
git status
```

**Requirements**:
- No build artifacts in staging
- No temporary files in staging
- Update .gitignore if needed
- Verify only intentional files staged

#### Step 6: Run Software Wrighter Checklist

```bash
sw-checklist
```

**Requirements**:
- ALL checklist items must pass
- Address any non-compliant project elements
- Run with --help for project-specific requirements

**If checklist fails**:
- Fix each failed requirement
- Re-run until all checks pass
- Update project structure as needed

**AI Agent Notes**:
- This ensures project meets Software Wrighter standards
- Use `sw-checklist --help` for detailed guidance
- Common checks: documentation, structure, licensing, quality

#### Step 7: Update Documentation

**CRITICAL**: If any of the previous steps required changes, update docs/learnings.md

**Update docs/learnings.md if**:
- Clippy warnings were found (document the pattern)
- Tests failed (document root cause)
- Bug was fixed (document prevention strategy)

**Root Cause Analysis Required**:
When updating learnings.md for bugs or test failures:
1. **What went wrong?** - Describe the issue
2. **Why wasn't it caught sooner?** - Identify process gap
3. **What process change prevents this?** - Document prevention
4. **Add to proactive checklist** - Update process.md if needed

**Also Update**:
- README.md if features added or changed
- CLAUDE.md if development patterns changed
- docs/status.md with progress
- docs/architecture.md if system design changed
- docs/design.md if design decisions made

#### Step 8: Final Review

**Self-Review Checklist**:
- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] Zero clippy warnings
- [ ] Code formatted
- [ ] Markdown validated (if applicable)
- [ ] .gitignore appropriate
- [ ] sw-checklist passes
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] docs/learnings.md updated if issues found
- [ ] No commented-out code
- [ ] No debug print statements
- [ ] Commit message clear and detailed

#### Step 9: Commit and Push
```bash
git add -A
git commit -m "Clear, descriptive message

Detailed explanation of changes...

If bugs were fixed:
- Root cause: <why the bug occurred>
- Prevention: <what process change prevents this>
- Updated learnings.md with <specific section>

[AI] Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>"

git push
```

**Commit Message Requirements**:
- Clear, descriptive summary (50 chars max)
- Detailed explanation of what and why
- Root cause analysis if bug fix
- Reference to learnings.md updates if applicable
- Co-authorship attribution

### 3. Quality Standards

**Code Quality**:
- Zero clippy warnings (enforced with `-D warnings`)
- All code formatted with `cargo fmt`
- Rust 2024 edition idioms
- Inline format arguments: `format!("{name}")` not `format!("{}", name)`
- Inner doc comments for modules: `//!` not `///` + empty line

**Test Coverage**:
- Unit tests for pure logic
- Integration tests for database operations
- UI tests for critical user flows
- Edge case handling

**Documentation**:
- Public APIs documented with `///` comments
- Module-level docs with `//!` comments
- README kept up-to-date
- Examples in doc comments where helpful

**Tech Debt Limits**:
- Maximum 3 TODO comments per file
- Files under 500 lines (prefer 200-300)
- Functions under 50 lines (prefer 10-30)
- Address TODOs within 2 development sessions
- Never commit FIXMEs (fix immediately)

## Build Process

### Local Development

**Initial Setup**:
```bash
# Install Rust (if needed)
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh

# Install wasm-pack
cargo install wasm-pack

# Verify prerequisites
./scripts/check-setup.sh
```

**Build**:
```bash
./scripts/build-all.sh
```

This script:
1. Builds Rust CLI (release mode)
2. Generates build-info.json
3. Builds WASM UI with wasm-pack

**Development Iteration**:
```bash
# Terminal 1: Run web server
./scripts/run-web.sh 2222

# Terminal 2: Make changes, then rebuild
./scripts/build-all.sh

# Refresh browser to see changes
```

**Testing**:
```bash
# Run all tests
cargo test

# Run specific test
cargo test test_name

# Run tests with output
cargo test -- --nocapture
```

### Continuous Integration

(Not yet implemented, but planned)

**GitHub Actions Workflow**:
```yaml
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v2
      - uses: actions-rs/toolchain@v1
      - run: cargo test
      - run: cargo clippy -- -D warnings
      - run: cargo fmt -- --check
```

## Testing Strategy

### Unit Tests

**Location**: Same file as code, in `#[cfg(test)]` module

**Example**:
```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn test_detect_rust_project() {
        let temp = tempfile::tempdir().unwrap();
        let cargo_toml = temp.path().join("Cargo.toml");
        fs::write(&cargo_toml, "[package]").unwrap();

        assert_eq!(detect_project_type(temp.path()), ProjectType::Rust);
    }
}
```

**What to Test**:
- Pure functions (no I/O)
- Edge cases (empty input, max values)
- Error conditions

### Integration Tests

**Location**: `src/lib.rs` in test module (access to internal API)

**Example**:
```rust
#[test]
fn test_project_crud() {
    let temp = tempfile::tempdir().unwrap();
    let db = Database::open_or_create_at(temp.path()).unwrap();

    let project = Project {
        name: "test-project".to_string(),
        // ...
    };

    let id = db.upsert_project(&project).unwrap();
    let retrieved = db.get_project(id).unwrap().unwrap();
    assert_eq!(retrieved.name, "test-project");
}
```

**What to Test**:
- Database operations
- Scanner integration (find + analyze + insert)
- API endpoints (future: with test harness)

### UI Tests

**Tool**: Playwright via MCP server

**Setup**:
```bash
# Install Playwright MCP
claude mcp add playwright -s user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp

# Verify
claude mcp list
```

**Example**:
```rust
// In Claude Code session:
// "Use Playwright to test the project detail view"
```

**What to Test**:
- Page loads correctly
- Table sorting works
- Search filtering works
- Detail view navigation
- Status update persistence

### Test Data Management

**Temporary Directories**:
```rust
use tempfile::tempdir;

let temp = tempdir()?;
// Create test data in temp.path()
// Automatic cleanup when temp drops
```

**Test Databases**:
```rust
let db = Database::open_or_create_at(temp.path())?;
// Fresh database for each test
// No shared state between tests
```

**Mock Data**:
```rust
fn create_test_project() -> Project {
    Project {
        id: 0,
        name: "test-project".to_string(),
        path: "/tmp/test".into(),
        // ...
    }
}
```

## Git Workflow

### Branch Strategy

**Current**: Direct commits to `main`
- Single developer
- Small changes
- Fast iteration

**Future** (Multi-developer):
- Feature branches: `feature/add-export`
- Bugfix branches: `fix/scanner-crash`
- PR-based review process

### Commit Messages

**Format**:
```
type: Short summary (50 chars max)

Detailed explanation of what changed and why.
Include context, rationale, and trade-offs.

Bullet points for multiple changes:
- Added feature X
- Fixed bug Y
- Refactored Z

[AI] Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
```

**Types**:
- `feat:` New feature
- `fix:` Bug fix
- `docs:` Documentation only
- `style:` Formatting, no code change
- `refactor:` Code restructuring
- `test:` Adding tests
- `chore:` Build process, dependencies

**Examples**:
```
feat: Add row numbers to project table

Added index column with small font for easy reference in
discussions. Updated CSS for compact styling.

fix: Correct refresh mechanism to capture DOM state

Changed refresh to re-check git status without full rescan.
Prevents stale data when user modifies projects externally.

docs: Add screenshot and fix markdown formatting

Replaced Unicode tree symbols with ASCII for portability.
Added full-page screenshot to README for visual context.
```

### Push Policy

**Always push immediately after commit**:
- Enables testing on other machines
- Provides backup of work
- Makes work visible for collaboration

**Never**:
- Force push to main (unless recovery needed)
- Rewrite history of pushed commits
- Skip pre-commit checks

## Code Review Guidelines

### Self-Review Checklist

Before committing, review your own changes:

- [ ] All tests pass
- [ ] No clippy warnings
- [ ] Code formatted
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] No hardcoded values (use constants)
- [ ] Error handling appropriate
- [ ] No commented-out code
- [ ] No debug print statements
- [ ] Commit message clear and detailed

### Common Issues to Watch For

From learnings.md:

1. **Doc comments**: Use `//!` for modules, `///` for items, no empty lines
2. **Unused imports**: Remove after refactoring
3. **Format arguments**: Use inline syntax `"{name}"` not `"{}", name`
4. **Needless borrows**: Trust clippy on generic args
5. **File size**: Keep under 500 lines
6. **TODO count**: Max 3 per file

## Deployment Process

### Pre-Release Checklist

- [ ] All tests passing
- [ ] Zero clippy warnings
- [ ] Code formatted
- [ ] README up-to-date with screenshots
- [ ] CHANGELOG.md updated
- [ ] Version bumped in Cargo.toml
- [ ] Git tag created: `v0.1.0`
- [ ] Build artifacts generated

### Release Steps

```bash
# 1. Update version
# Edit Cargo.toml: version = "0.1.0"

# 2. Update CHANGELOG
# Document all changes since last release

# 3. Commit version bump
git add Cargo.toml CHANGELOG.md
git commit -m "chore: Bump version to 0.1.0"

# 4. Create tag
git tag -a v0.1.0 -m "Release v0.1.0"

# 5. Push
git push && git push --tags

# 6. Build release artifacts
./scripts/build-all.sh
tar -czf needs-attention-v0.1.0-macos.tar.gz \
    target/release/needs-attention \
    static/ \
    wasm-ui/pkg/

# 7. Create GitHub release
# Upload tar.gz, add release notes from CHANGELOG
```

## Maintenance

### Dependency Updates

**Monthly Check**:
```bash
cargo update
cargo test
cargo clippy
# If all pass, commit Cargo.lock update
```

**Breaking Changes**:
- Review changelog for dependencies
- Test thoroughly after major version bumps
- Update code if APIs changed

### Database Migrations

**Current**: Ignored ALTER TABLE statements

**Future**: Proper migration system
```bash
# Track version in database
# Run migrations on startup
# Provide rollback mechanism
```

## Tooling

### Required Tools

- Rust (latest stable)
- wasm-pack
- git

### Recommended Tools

- markdown-checker (for docs)
- Playwright (for UI testing)
- cargo-watch (for auto-rebuild)

### Optional Tools

- cargo-bloat (analyze binary size)
- cargo-audit (security vulnerabilities)
- cargo-outdated (dependency updates)

## Emergency Procedures

### Rollback

```bash
# Undo last commit (not pushed)
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Undo last commit (pushed)
# Create revert commit instead
git revert HEAD
git push
```

### Database Corruption

```bash
# Restore from backup
cp needs_attention.db.backup needs_attention.db

# Or recreate
rm needs_attention.db
./scripts/gather.sh /path/to/projects
```

### Build Failures

```bash
# Clean build artifacts
cargo clean
rm -rf wasm-ui/pkg
./scripts/build-all.sh

# Reset to known-good commit
git log --oneline
git checkout <commit-sha>
```

## Continuous Improvement

### Learning Integration

1. Document issues in learnings.md
2. Update process.md with prevention strategies
3. Add to checkpoint checklist if recurring
4. Consider automation for frequent issues

### Metrics Tracking

(Future implementation)

**Track**:
- Build times
- Test execution time
- Code coverage
- Clippy warnings over time
- Database size growth

**Tools**:
- cargo bench for performance
- tarpaulin for coverage
- GitHub Actions for CI metrics

content.content:
1	# Development Tools
2	
3	This document describes the recommended tools for working with this project, particularly for AI coding agents and developers.
4	
5	## Tool Location
6	
7	Recommended tools are installed in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`:
8	
9	```bash
10	# List available tools
11	ls -la ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
12	
13	# Add to PATH (if not already in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc)
14	export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
15	```
16	
17	## Tool Help Convention
18	
19	**IMPORTANT**: All tools in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/` support `--help` with AI coding agent-specific sections. These extended help sections provide additional context, examples, and best practices tailored for AI agents.
20	
21	```bash
22	# Standard help (short)
23	tool-name -h
24	
25	# Extended help with AI sections (recommended)
26	tool-name --help
27	```
28	
29	## Core Tools
30	
31	### 1. proact - AI Agent Documentation Generator
32	
33	**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/proact
34	**Version**: 0.1.0
35	
36	**Purpose**: Generates comprehensive AI coding agent documentation for projects
37	
38	**When to Use**:
39	- When starting work on a new project
40	- When you want standardized AI agent instructions
41	- When docs/learnings.md needs to be initialized or updated
42	- When project processes/standards have changed
43	
44	**Installation**:
45	```bash
46	# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
47	```
48	
49	**Usage**:
50	```bash
51	# Generate docs for current project
52	proact .
53	
54	# Generate docs for specific project
55	proact /path/to/project
56	
57	# Custom output directory
58	proact /path/to/project -o documentation
59	
60	# Preview what would be generated (dry-run)
61	proact . --dry-run
62	
63	# Verbose output
64	proact . --verbose
65	```
66	
67	**What It Generates**:
68	- `docs/ai_agent_instructions.md` - Comprehensive AI coding agent guidelines
69	  - Process-oriented workflow
70	  - Quality standards
71	  - Testing strategies
72	  - Tech debt avoidance
73	  - Playwright MCP setup
74	  - Project-specific guidelines (Rust, WASM, etc.)
75	- `docs/learnings.md` - Template for capturing issues and solutions
76	  - Common patterns that cause problems
77	  - Proactive prevention strategies
78	  - Root cause analysis examples
79	
80	**Key Features**:
81	- Enforces checkpoint process (tests, linting, formatting, docs, git)
82	- Includes Playwright MCP setup instructions
83	- Project-specific guidelines based on technology detected
84	- Continuous improvement focus
85	- Tech debt limits (file size, TODO count, etc.)
86	
87	**AI Agent Notes**:
88	When proact generates documentation for a project, it creates a baseline. You should:
89	1. Read the generated docs/ai_agent_instructions.md
90	2. Follow the checkpoint process it describes
91	3. Update docs/learnings.md when you encounter issues
92	4. Run proact again if project structure changes significantly
93	
94	### 2. markdown-checker - Markdown Validation Tool
95	
96	**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/markdown-checker
97	**Version**: 0.1.0
98	
99	**Purpose**: Ensures markdown files use ASCII-only characters for maximum portability
100	
101	**When to Use**:
102	- BEFORE committing any markdown changes (part of pre-commit process)
103	- After editing README.md or any docs/*.md files
104	- When preparing documentation for GitHub (web preview compatibility)
105	- To fix Unicode characters (arrows, emojis, box-drawing)
106	
107	**Installation**:
108	```bash
109	# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
110	```
111	
112	**Usage**:
113	```bash
114	# Validate README.md in current directory
115	markdown-checker
116	
117	# Validate specific file
118	markdown-checker -f CONTRIBUTING.md
119	
120	# Validate all markdown in directory
121	markdown-checker -f "*.md"
122	
123	# Validate recursively (all markdown in docs/)
124	markdown-checker -p docs -f "**/*.md"
125	
126	# Auto-fix tree symbols
127	markdown-checker --fix
128	
129	# Preview fixes without applying
130	markdown-checker --dry-run
131	
132	# Verbose output
133	markdown-checker -v
134	```
135	
136	**Validation Rules**:
137	- [x] ASCII characters (32-126)
138	- [x] Whitespace (space, tab, LF, CR)
139	- [x] Unicode arrows (-> becomes ->)
140	- [x] Emojis ([x] becomes [x] or remove)
141	- [x] Box-drawing characters (+|+- becomes +|-)
142	
143	**Auto-Fixable**:
144	- Tree symbols: `+ -> +`, `| -> |`, `+ -> +`, `- -> -`
145	
146	**Not Auto-Fixable** (manual intervention required):
147	- Unicode arrows (->)
148	- Emojis ([x] [x] [WIP])
149	- Accented characters (e a n)
150	- Other non-ASCII Unicode
151	
152	**Why ASCII-Only?**:
153	- GitHub README web preview works correctly
154	- Compatible with all terminals and editors
155	- Works in CI/CD pipelines
156	- Accessible to screen readers
157	- No encoding issues
158	
159	**Pre-Commit Integration**:
160	```bash
161	# Part of mandatory pre-commit process
162	markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
163	# Fix any issues before committing
164	```
165	
166	**AI Agent Notes**:
167	- ALWAYS run markdown-checker before committing documentation
168	- Use --fix for tree symbols, manually fix other Unicode
169	- If validation fails, FIX the issues (never skip this step)
170	- Common fixes:
171	  - `->` becomes `->`
172	  - `[x]` becomes `[x]` in checklists
173	  - `[WIP]` becomes `[WIP]` or remove
174	  - Box-drawing trees become ASCII trees
175	
176	### 3. sw-install - Binary Installation Tool
177	
178	**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/sw-install
179	**Version**: 0.1.0
180	
181	**Purpose**: Installs compiled Rust binaries to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`
182	
183	**When to Use**:
184	- First-time setup of development environment
185	- After building a new Rust CLI tool locally
186	- When updating an existing tool
187	- When you need a specific version of a tool
188	
189	**Installation**:
190	```bash
191	# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
192	```
193	
194	**First-Time Setup**:
195	```bash
196	# 1. Setup installation directory and PATH
197	sw-install --setup-install-dir
198	
199	# 2. Reload shell configuration
200	source ~/.bashrc  # or ~/.zshrc
201	```
202	
203	**Usage**:
204	```bash
205	# Install a binary from project (release build)
206	sw-install -p ~/projects/markdown-checker
207	
208	# Install with custom name
209	sw-install -p ~/projects/ask -r ask-dev
210	
211	# Install debug build (for development)
212	sw-install -p ~/projects/proact -d
213	
214	# Uninstall a binary
215	sw-install -u markdown-checker
216	
217	# Dry-run (preview what would happen)
218	sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --dry-run
219	
220	# Verbose output
221	sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --verbose
222	```
223	
224	**How It Works**:
225	1. Detects Rust project (looks for Cargo.toml)
226	2. Finds binary name from Cargo.toml [[bin]] section
227	3. Copies from `target/release/` or `target/debug/`
228	4. Installs to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`
229	5. Makes executable (chmod +x)
230	
231	**PATH Configuration**:
232	After `--setup-install-dir`, adds to your shell RC file:
233	```bash
234	export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
235	```

# Development Tools

This document describes the recommended tools for working with this project, particularly for AI coding agents and developers.

## Tool Location

Recommended tools are installed in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`:

```bash
# List available tools
ls -la ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/

# Add to PATH (if not already in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc)
export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
```

## Tool Help Convention

**IMPORTANT**: All tools in `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/` support `--help` with AI coding agent-specific sections. These extended help sections provide additional context, examples, and best practices tailored for AI agents.

```bash
# Standard help (short)
tool-name -h

# Extended help with AI sections (recommended)
tool-name --help
```

## Core Tools

### 1. proact - AI Agent Documentation Generator

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/proact
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Generates comprehensive AI coding agent documentation for projects

**When to Use**:
- When starting work on a new project
- When you want standardized AI agent instructions
- When docs/learnings.md needs to be initialized or updated
- When project processes/standards have changed

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Generate docs for current project
proact .

# Generate docs for specific project
proact /path/to/project

# Custom output directory
proact /path/to/project -o documentation

# Preview what would be generated (dry-run)
proact . --dry-run

# Verbose output
proact . --verbose
```

**What It Generates**:
- `docs/ai_agent_instructions.md` - Comprehensive AI coding agent guidelines
  - Process-oriented workflow
  - Quality standards
  - Testing strategies
  - Tech debt avoidance
  - Playwright MCP setup
  - Project-specific guidelines (Rust, WASM, etc.)
- `docs/learnings.md` - Template for capturing issues and solutions
  - Common patterns that cause problems
  - Proactive prevention strategies
  - Root cause analysis examples

**Key Features**:
- Enforces checkpoint process (tests, linting, formatting, docs, git)
- Includes Playwright MCP setup instructions
- Project-specific guidelines based on technology detected
- Continuous improvement focus
- Tech debt limits (file size, TODO count, etc.)

**AI Agent Notes**:
When proact generates documentation for a project, it creates a baseline. You should:
1. Read the generated docs/ai_agent_instructions.md
2. Follow the checkpoint process it describes
3. Update docs/learnings.md when you encounter issues
4. Run proact again if project structure changes significantly

### 2. markdown-checker - Markdown Validation Tool

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/markdown-checker
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Ensures markdown files use ASCII-only characters for maximum portability

**When to Use**:
- BEFORE committing any markdown changes (part of pre-commit process)
- After editing README.md or any docs/*.md files
- When preparing documentation for GitHub (web preview compatibility)
- To fix Unicode characters (arrows, emojis, box-drawing)

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Validate README.md in current directory
markdown-checker

# Validate specific file
markdown-checker -f CONTRIBUTING.md

# Validate all markdown in directory
markdown-checker -f "*.md"

# Validate recursively (all markdown in docs/)
markdown-checker -p docs -f "**/*.md"

# Auto-fix tree symbols
markdown-checker --fix

# Preview fixes without applying
markdown-checker --dry-run

# Verbose output
markdown-checker -v
```

**Validation Rules**:
- [x] ASCII characters (32-126)
- [x] Whitespace (space, tab, LF, CR)
- [x] Unicode arrows (-> becomes ->)
- [x] Emojis ([x] becomes [x] or remove)
- [x] Box-drawing characters (+|+- becomes +|-)

**Auto-Fixable**:
- Tree symbols: `+ -> +`, `| -> |`, `+ -> +`, `- -> -`

**Not Auto-Fixable** (manual intervention required):
- Unicode arrows (->)
- Emojis ([x] [x] [WIP])
- Accented characters (e a n)
- Other non-ASCII Unicode

**Why ASCII-Only?**:
- GitHub README web preview works correctly
- Compatible with all terminals and editors
- Works in CI/CD pipelines
- Accessible to screen readers
- No encoding issues

**Pre-Commit Integration**:
```bash
# Part of mandatory pre-commit process
markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"
# Fix any issues before committing
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- ALWAYS run markdown-checker before committing documentation
- Use --fix for tree symbols, manually fix other Unicode
- If validation fails, FIX the issues (never skip this step)
- Common fixes:
  - `->` becomes `->`
  - `[x]` becomes `[x]` in checklists
  - `[WIP]` becomes `[WIP]` or remove
  - Box-drawing trees become ASCII trees

### 3. sw-install - Binary Installation Tool

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/sw-install
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Installs compiled Rust binaries to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`

**When to Use**:
- First-time setup of development environment
- After building a new Rust CLI tool locally
- When updating an existing tool
- When you need a specific version of a tool

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**First-Time Setup**:
```bash
# 1. Setup installation directory and PATH
sw-install --setup-install-dir

# 2. Reload shell configuration
source ~/.bashrc  # or ~/.zshrc
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Install a binary from project (release build)
sw-install -p ~/projects/markdown-checker

# Install with custom name
sw-install -p ~/projects/ask -r ask-dev

# Install debug build (for development)
sw-install -p ~/projects/proact -d

# Uninstall a binary
sw-install -u markdown-checker

# Dry-run (preview what would happen)
sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --dry-run

# Verbose output
sw-install -p ~/projects/ask --verbose
```

**How It Works**:
1. Detects Rust project (looks for Cargo.toml)
2. Finds binary name from Cargo.toml [[bin]] section
3. Copies from `target/release/` or `target/debug/`
4. Installs to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`
5. Makes executable (chmod +x)

**PATH Configuration**:
After `--setup-install-dir`, adds to your shell RC file:
```bash
export PATH="$HOME/.local/softwarewrighter/bin:$PATH"
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- Use sw-install when you build a new tool and want it available globally
- Prefer release builds for performance: `sw-install -p <path>`
- Use debug builds for development: `sw-install -p <path> -d`
- Check which tools are available: `ls ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`

### 4. ask - Command-Line LLM Query Tool

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/ask
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Query language models from the command line for quick assistance

**When to Use**:
- Need quick answers without opening a full AI interface

- Prototyping prompts

# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Query with default settings (Ollama, phi3:3.8b)
ask "How do I parse JSON in Rust?"

# Use a specific platform and model
ask -p openai -m gpt-4 "Explain async/await in Rust"

# Use shortcut for platform/model combo
ask -s  # List available shortcuts
ask -s sonnet "Write a bash script to..."

# Dry run (show request without calling API)
ask -n "Test query"

# Verbose output
ask -v "Debug query"

# Show usage statistics
ask --usage-report
```

**Supported Platforms**:
- Ollama (default, local)
- OpenAI (requires API key)
- Anthropic (requires API key)

**Configuration**:
```bash
# API keys via environment variables
export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."

# Or via config file

**AI Agent Notes**:

- Ollama is default (runs locally, no API costs)

### 5. sw-checklist - Software Wrighter Project Requirements Checker

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/sw-checklist
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Ensures that a project meets Software Wrighter standards and requirements

**When to Use**:
- Before committing changes to verify project compliance
- When setting up a new project to ensure all requirements are met
- During pre-commit process to validate project structure
- To identify missing or non-compliant project elements

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:

# Run checklist on current project

# Show extended help with AI agent guidance
sw-checklist --help

# Run with verbose output

# Dry run (preview checks without modifications)
sw-checklist --dry-run
```

**What It Checks**:

- Documentation completeness
- Code quality standards

**AI Agent Notes**:

### 6. favicon - Favicon Generator

**Repository**: https://github.com/softwarewrighter/favicon
**Version**: 0.1.0

**Purpose**: Generates favicon.ico files for web projects

**When to Use**:
- Setting up a new web project that needs a favicon
- Creating placeholder favicons during development
- Generating production-ready favicon files

**Installation**:
```bash
# Already installed in ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
```

**Usage**:
```bash
# Show extended help with AI agent guidance
favicon --help

# Generate red question mark on transparent background (placeholder)
# Run this in the directory where index.html is served (e.g., public/ or static/)
favicon -T -t "?" -b ff0000

# Common options:
# -T          : Transparent background
# -t "TEXT"   : Text to render in favicon
# -b RRGGBB   : Background color (hex)
# -f RRGGBB   : Foreground/text color (hex)
```

**Common Patterns**:

```bash
# Placeholder during development (red question mark)
cd public  # or wherever index.html is served
favicon -T -t "?" -b ff0000

# Development marker (yellow "D")
favicon -T -t "D" -b ffff00 -f 000000

# Staging marker (orange "S")
favicon -T -t "S" -b ff8800 -f ffffff

# Production placeholder (blue "P")

```

**AI Agent Notes**:

- Common locations: public/, static/, dist/, www/
- Replace placeholder favicons before production deployment
- Use distinctive colors/text for different environments (dev/staging/prod)

## Tool Discovery

When new tools are added to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`:

1. **List available tools**:
   ```bash
   ls -la ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/
   ```

2. **Check tool help**:

   ```

   ```

4. **Update this doc** when new useful tools are discovered

## Integration with This Project

### Pre-Commit Process

From docs/process.md, the following tools are used:

```bash
# Step 1: Tests
cargo test

# Step 2: Linting
cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings

# Step 3: Formatting

# Step 4: Markdown validation (THIS IS WHERE markdown-checker IS USED)
markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"

# Step 6: Update documentation
# If issues found, update docs/learnings.md
# Can use proact to regenerate if major changes
```

### Documentation Workflow

```bash
# 1. Make code/doc changes
vim README.md

# 2. Validate markdown before commit
markdown-checker -f "README.md"

# 3. Fix any issues
# (manually replace Unicode, or use --fix for tree symbols)

# 4. If new patterns found, update docs/learnings.md

# 5. Optionally regenerate full docs
proact .

# 6. Commit changes
git commit -m "docs: Update README with new features"
```

## Best Practices

### For AI Coding Agents

1. **Always check tool help first**:
   ```bash
   <tool> --help  # Not -h, use --help for AI-specific sections
   ```

2. **Use dry-run mode when learning**:
   ```bash
   proact . --dry-run
   markdown-checker --dry-run
   ```

3. **Integrate into workflow**:
   - Use markdown-checker in pre-commit process (mandatory)

   - Update docs/learnings.md when encountering issues

4. **Validate before committing**:
   ```bash
   # Always run before commit if markdown changed

   ```

5. **Keep tools updated**:
   ```bash
   # Rebuild and reinstall tool
   cd ~/projects/markdown-checker
   cargo build --release

   ```

### For Human Developers

1. **Setup PATH once**:
   ```bash
   sw-install --setup-install-dir

   ```

2. **Install tools as needed**:
   ```bash

   # Install globally
   sw-install -p .
   ```

3. **Use in pre-commit hooks** (future):
   ```bash
   # .git/hooks/pre-commit
   #!/bin/bash
   markdown-checker -f "**/*.md" || exit 1
   ```

## Troubleshooting

### Tool Not Found

```bash
# Check if tool exists
ls ~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/

# Check if PATH is set
echo $PATH | grep softwarewrighter

# Re-run setup if needed

### markdown-checker Validation Failures

```bash

# Try auto-fix for tree symbols

# -> with ->
# [x] with [x]
# [WIP] with [WIP] or remove
```

### Permission Denied

```bash

```

## Future Tools

As new tools are added to `~/.local/softwarewrighter/bin/`, document them here:

**Template**:
```markdown
### Tool Name

**Purpose**: Brief description

**When to Use**:
- Use case 1
- Use case 2

**Usage**:
```bash
tool-name --help
tool-name <common-usage>
```

**AI Agent Notes**:
- Specific guidance for AI agents
- Integration with project workflow
```

37	- Clearly communicate the rationale for each decision in the commit message or separate notes.
38	
39	### f. Git Operations
40	- Stage changes with `git add`.
41	- Create a logical commit with a clear, descriptive message summarizing the checkpoint scope.
42	- Push the commit immediately (`git push`) to the remote repository, ensuring incremental backups and availability for testing and review on other systems.
43	
44	---
45	
46	## 2. Quality-Oriented Development
47	
48	Ensure all generated code adheres strictly to the following quality standards:
49	
50	### Documentation
51	- Clearly document all code modules, functions, and key logic blocks.
52	- Ensure compatibility with automated documentation generation tools (`cargo doc`, `Sphinx`, `JSDoc`, etc.).
53	- Write and maintain accurate, relevant doc tests within code comments to demonstrate correct usage.
54	
55	### Testing
56	- Include unit tests for individual functions, validating core logic and handling edge cases.
57	- Provide functional tests verifying code behavior meets defined requirements and user stories.
58	- Integrate code modules through comprehensive integration tests confirming interoperability and end-to-end correctness.
59	- For Rust/WASM projects: Write tests in Rust, not Python. Use `wasm-bindgen-test` for WASM-specific tests.
60	- Explicitly defer more advanced testing (stress, load, benchmarks, fuzz testing) until explicitly requested by the developer.
61	
62	### Tech Debt Avoidance
63

65	- Keep source files under 500 lines (prefer 200-300 lines)

67	- If a file exceeds 500 lines, refactor immediately
68	
69	**TODO Comments**:
70	- Limit to 3 TODO comments per file maximum
71	- Address TODOs within 2 development sessions
72	- Convert persistent TODOs to GitHub issues
73	- Never commit code with FIXMEs - resolve immediately
74	
75	**Code Complexity**:
76	- Functions should be under 50 lines (prefer 10-30)
77	- Cyclomatic complexity should stay below 10
78	- Deeply nested code (>3 levels) indicates need for refactoring
79	
80	**Dependencies**:
81	- Audit dependencies regularly - remove unused ones
82	- Prefer well-maintained, popular crates/packages
83	- Minimize dependency tree depth
84	
85	### Scripting Guidelines
86	
87	**Bash Scripts**:
88	- Use bash for simple automation (< 100 lines)
89	- Use shellcheck for validation
90	- Always use `set -euo pipefail` at the start
91	- Quote all variables: `"${var}"` not `$var`
92	
93	**When to Switch to Python**:

100	**Python Scripts**:

103	- Use `argparse` for CLI arguments
104	- Keep scripts focused - one purpose per script
105	
106	---

110	Actively integrate learning from previous mistakes into the proactive development cycle to prevent recurring errors:
111	
112	### Build and Test Failures
113	- Document all encountered build and test failures with specific causes.
114	- Regularly review failure logs and integrate solutions into the standard development workflow proactively.
115	- Maintain a growing reference of common issues and their resolutions for quick reference.
116	
117	### Linting and Static Analysis
118	- Track common linting warnings and static analysis issues encountered historically.
119	- Proactively avoid patterns that have caused past warnings, incorporating this knowledge into the code generation and review phases.
120	
121	### Process Documentation Updates

123	- Clearly specify new guidelines or modifications to existing ones to eliminate similar future problems.
124	- Use precise examples from historical failures to illustrate improved practices clearly.
125	
126	---
127	
128	## 4. Playwright MCP Server Setup and Usage
129	
130	### Overview
131	
132	Playwright MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a server that provides browser automation capabilities to AI coding assistants. It enables interaction with web pages through structured accessibility snapshots, bypassing the need for screenshots or visually-tuned models.
133	
134	### Key Benefits
135	- **Accessibility-tree based**: Uses Playwright's accessibility tree instead of pixel-based input
136	- **Deterministic execution**: Avoids ambiguity common with screenshot-based approaches
137	- **No vision models required**: Operates purely on structured data
138	- **Cross-platform support**: Works with multiple AI coding assistants through standardized MCP protocol
139	
140	### Installation Instructions
141	
142	#### For Claude Code
143	```bash
144	# User-level installation (recommended - available across all projects)
145	claude mcp add playwright -s user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp
146	
147	# Verify installation
148	claude mcp list
149	```
150	
151	#### For Gemini CLI
152	```bash
153	# Ensure Node.js v20+ is installed
154	# Configure Playwright MCP server
155	npx @playwright/mcp@latest
156	```
157	
158	#### For OpenCode

160	```json
161	{
162	  "mcpServers": {
163	    "playwright": {

167	      "env": []
168	    }
169	  }
170	}
171	```

175	- Claude Code: `mcp__playwright__[action]`

177

180	- **Interaction**: Click elements, fill forms, select options, drag and drop
181	- **State Management**: Handle dialogs, evaluate JavaScript, manage tabs

183	- **Waiting**: Wait for specific elements or conditions

# AI Coding Agent Development Process Guidelines

This document provides explicit guidelines to be proactively followed by AI coding agents (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, opencode, Codex, etc.) during development tasks. Adherence ensures consistency, quality, maintainability, and continual improvement in codebases.

---

## 1. Process-Oriented Workflow

When a **checkpoint** is requested by the developer, execute the following sequence rigorously:

### a. Run and Fix Failing Tests
- Execute all available tests (unit, functional, integration).
- Identify failing tests and systematically debug and correct them.
- Confirm all tests pass post-fix.

### b. Fix Linting Issues
- Run appropriate linters for the project language (e.g., `clippy` for Rust, `eslint` for JavaScript).
- Resolve all warnings and errors, prioritizing idiomatic and efficient code solutions.
- Re-run linters to ensure clean results.

### c. Format Source Code
- Use standard formatting tools (e.g., `rustfmt`, `prettier`, `black`) to format all source files consistently.
- Ensure no formatting warnings or changes remain pending.

### d. Update Documentation
- Review affected documentation.
- Update comments, README files, and other documentation to reflect all code changes accurately.
- Include meaningful descriptions, usage examples, and doc tests as appropriate.

### e. Manage Git Status
- Run `git status` to identify changes.
- Categorize each file:
  - Stage for commit (`git add`) if relevant to the logical checkpoint.
  - Ignore/untrack irrelevant or temporary files via `.gitignore`.
  - Delete or move irrelevant files into an explicitly `.gitignored` folder when appropriate.
- Clearly communicate the rationale for each decision in the commit message or separate notes.

### f. Git Operations
- Stage changes with `git add`.
- Create a logical commit with a clear, descriptive message summarizing the checkpoint scope.
- Push the commit immediately (`git push`) to the remote repository, ensuring incremental backups and availability for testing and review on other systems.

---

## 2. Quality-Oriented Development

Ensure all generated code adheres strictly to the following quality standards:

### Documentation
- Clearly document all code modules, functions, and key logic blocks.
- Ensure compatibility with automated documentation generation tools (`cargo doc`, `Sphinx`, `JSDoc`, etc.).
- Write and maintain accurate, relevant doc tests within code comments to demonstrate correct usage.

### Testing
- Include unit tests for individual functions, validating core logic and handling edge cases.
- Provide functional tests verifying code behavior meets defined requirements and user stories.
- Integrate code modules through comprehensive integration tests confirming interoperability and end-to-end correctness.
- For Rust/WASM projects: Write tests in Rust, not Python. Use `wasm-bindgen-test` for WASM-specific tests.
- Explicitly defer more advanced testing (stress, load, benchmarks, fuzz testing) until explicitly requested by the developer.

### Tech Debt Avoidance

**File Size Limits**:
- Keep source files under 500 lines (prefer 200-300 lines)
- Split large files into logical modules
- If a file exceeds 500 lines, refactor immediately

**TODO Comments**:
- Limit to 3 TODO comments per file maximum
- Address TODOs within 2 development sessions
- Convert persistent TODOs to GitHub issues
- Never commit code with FIXMEs - resolve immediately

**Code Complexity**:
- Functions should be under 50 lines (prefer 10-30)
- Cyclomatic complexity should stay below 10
- Deeply nested code (>3 levels) indicates need for refactoring

**Dependencies**:
- Audit dependencies regularly - remove unused ones
- Prefer well-maintained, popular crates/packages
- Minimize dependency tree depth

### Scripting Guidelines

**Bash Scripts**:
- Use bash for simple automation (< 100 lines)
- Use shellcheck for validation
- Always use `set -euo pipefail` at the start
- Quote all variables: `"${var}"` not `$var`

**When to Switch to Python**:
- Script exceeds 100 lines
- Complex data structures needed
- JSON/YAML parsing required
- Cross-platform compatibility needed
- Error handling becomes complex

**Python Scripts**:
- Use type hints even in scripts
- Include `#!/usr/bin/env python3` shebang
- Use `argparse` for CLI arguments
- Keep scripts focused - one purpose per script

---

## 3. Continuous Improvement

Actively integrate learning from previous mistakes into the proactive development cycle to prevent recurring errors:

### Build and Test Failures
- Document all encountered build and test failures with specific causes.
- Regularly review failure logs and integrate solutions into the standard development workflow proactively.
- Maintain a growing reference of common issues and their resolutions for quick reference.

### Linting and Static Analysis
- Track common linting warnings and static analysis issues encountered historically.
- Proactively avoid patterns that have caused past warnings, incorporating this knowledge into the code generation and review phases.

### Process Documentation Updates
- Immediately update process documentation whenever you encounter and solve recurring build, test, or lint issues.
- Clearly specify new guidelines or modifications to existing ones to eliminate similar future problems.
- Use precise examples from historical failures to illustrate improved practices clearly.

---

## 4. Playwright MCP Server Setup and Usage

### Overview

Playwright MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a server that provides browser automation capabilities to AI coding assistants. It enables interaction with web pages through structured accessibility snapshots, bypassing the need for screenshots or visually-tuned models.

### Key Benefits
- **Accessibility-tree based**: Uses Playwright's accessibility tree instead of pixel-based input
- **Deterministic execution**: Avoids ambiguity common with screenshot-based approaches
- **No vision models required**: Operates purely on structured data
- **Cross-platform support**: Works with multiple AI coding assistants through standardized MCP protocol

### Installation Instructions

#### For Claude Code
```bash
# User-level installation (recommended - available across all projects)
claude mcp add playwright -s user -- npx -y @playwright/mcp

# Verify installation
claude mcp list
```

#### For Gemini CLI
```bash
# Ensure Node.js v20+ is installed
# Configure Playwright MCP server
npx @playwright/mcp@latest
```

#### For OpenCode
Configure in the MCP settings file:
```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "type": "stdio",
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@playwright/mcp"],
      "env": []
    }
  }
}
```

### Tool Naming Convention
All Playwright MCP tools follow a consistent naming pattern:
- Claude Code: `mcp__playwright__[action]`
- Other clients may use different prefixes based on their MCP implementation

### Available Tools
- **Navigation**: Navigate to URLs, go back/forward in history
- **Interaction**: Click elements, fill forms, select options, drag and drop
- **State Management**: Handle dialogs, evaluate JavaScript, manage tabs
- **Inspection**: Take screenshots, capture accessibility snapshots, view console logs
- **Waiting**: Wait for specific elements or conditions

### Best Practices
1. **Use User-Level Setup**: Configure frequently-used MCP servers at user level for consistency
2. **Project-Level for Specific Needs**: Use project-level configuration only for project-specific tools
3. **Regular Updates**: Periodically update the MCP server to get latest features
4. **Consistent Naming**: Always use "playwright" as the server name for consistency

### Troubleshooting

#### Browser Installation
If you encounter browser-related errors:
```bash
# Install Chromium (recommended)
npx playwright install chromium

# Or install all browsers
npx playwright install
```

#### Verification
After setup, verify the connection:
```bash
# For Claude Code
claude
> /mcp

# Should show: playwright: npx -y @playwright/mcp - ✓ Connected
```

---


## Summary of Expected Proactive Behaviors

- Always anticipate checkpoints and proactively align code with defined processes.
- Prioritize incremental, clean commits with immediate pushes to maintain transparency and availability.
- Document thoroughly, test rigorously, format consistently, and manage version control meticulously.
- Continuously adapt and integrate historical lessons into proactive workflow adjustments to minimize repetitive errors.
- Utilize MCP servers like Playwright for enhanced capabilities when working with web automation tasks.

By diligently following these guidelines, AI coding agents will significantly enhance development efficiency, maintainability, and overall code quality.

---

*Generated by Proact - AI Coding Agent Documentation Generator*

content.content:
1	
2	
3	
4	
5	Is ocaml open source (or are there open source implementations of it)?  How hard would it be to create from scratch?  Would it be best to implement it in C or assembler or other language?  Does it bootstrap itself like lisp or forth?
6	
7	I’m checking the current OCaml licensing, implementation status, and bootstrap details so I can answer the “open source / from scratch / implementation language / self-hosting” parts accurately.
8	
9	I’ve confirmed the current upstream state: OCaml itself is open source, the core system still mixes OCaml and C, and it remains a bootstrapped compiler rather than a tiny “write it all in assembly” system. I’m pulling one more level of detail on the bootstrap path so I can answer the self-hosting part cleanly.
10	
11	Thought for 22s
12	Yes.
13	
14	Restating the question
15	You are asking four separate things:
16	
17	Is OCaml open source?
18	
19	Are there open-source implementations of OCaml?
20	
21	How hard would it be to create one from scratch?
22	
23	What language is best for an implementation, and is it self-hosting/bootstraped like Lisp or Forth?
24	
25	The current state
26	The answer to the first two is yes. The main upstream OCaml implementation is open source, published by the OCaml project, with source code in the public ocaml/ocaml repository. The official site also lists current public releases; as of now it shows OCaml 5.4.1 as the latest release and 4.14.3 as the LTS release. 
27	
28	The upstream implementation is not a toy interpreter. It includes:
29	
30	a bytecode compiler
31	
32	a bytecode runtime/interpreter written in C
33	
34	a native-code compiler
35	
36	the runtime system and standard libraries 
37	
38	So the main implementation is already open source, and there are also public forks and variants built from it. The important point is that you do not need to hunt for some separate “open” implementation: the primary one is already open. 
39	
40	Is it self-hosting?
41	Mostly yes, but not in the pure Lisp/Forth sense.
42	
43	The OCaml compiler is bootstrapped. The project’s own hacking docs say the compiler is bootstrapped and that previously compiled bytecode versions of the compiler and lexer are kept in the repository under boot/. Those are used after the C bytecode runtime is built, and then a fresh compiler is built from there. 
44	
45	That means:
46	
47	the compiler is substantially written in OCaml
48	
49	but it still depends on a runtime written in C
50	
51	and the initial build uses prebuilt bootstrap artifacts rather than “compile from handwritten source using only assembly” 
52	
53	So compared with your examples:
54	
55	More self-hosting than many C-family compilers in practice
56	
57	Less minimal/purely circular than classic small Lisp or Forth systems that can often be grown from a tiny seed interactively
58	
59	How hard would it be to create from scratch?
60	High-level answer
61	Creating a real OCaml implementation from scratch is hard, but not equally hard at all levels.
62	
63	There are three realistic scopes:
64	
65	1. Tiny educational subset
66	This is very doable.
67	
68	For example:
69	
70	integers, booleans, tuples
71	
72	let, fun, application
73	
74	algebraic data types
75	
76	pattern matching
77	
78	Hindley–Milner type inference
79	
80	bytecode or tree-walk interpreter
81	
82	That is a solid language project.
83	
84	2. “Looks like OCaml” but incomplete
85	This is a medium-to-large project.
86	
87	You might add:
88	
89	modules
90	
91	records and variants
92	
93	exceptions
94	
95	recursive definitions
96	
97	some standard library
98	
99	separate compilation
100	
101	Still feasible for one person over time.
102	
103	3. Real OCaml compatibility
104	This is a major compiler/runtime project.
105	
106	What makes it hard is not just parsing. The hard parts are:
107	
108	full type inference and typing rules
109	
110	module system
111	
112	pattern-match compilation
113	
114	closures and calling conventions
115	
116	garbage collector
117	
118	exceptions/runtime representation
119	
120	separate compilation and interfaces
121	
122	native code generation or a serious VM
123	
124	compatibility with existing OCaml semantics and ecosystem
125	
126	That is much closer to “build a production compiler” than “write a language toy.”
127	
128	Architecture considerations
129	If your goal is educational, there are two sensible designs.
130	
131	Option A: Interpreter first
132	Best if you want to understand semantics.
133	
134	lexer
135	
136	parser
137	
138	AST
139	
140	type inference
141	
142	evaluator
143	
144	small runtime
145	
146	Pros
147	
148	fastest path to something working
149	
150	easiest to debug
151	
152	ideal for learning
153	
154	Cons
155	
156	not very OCaml-like in performance
157	
158	less interesting if your goal is code generation
159	
160	Option B: Typed AST to bytecode / custom VM
161	Best if you want a real implementation path.
162	
163	lexer
164	
165	parser
166	
167	typed IR
168	
169	closure conversion or direct lambda IR
170	
171	bytecode compiler
172	
173	C or Rust VM
174	
175	optional native compiler later
176	
177	Pros
178	
179	scales better
180	
181	closer to real compiler engineering
182	
183	easier later to target native code
184	
185	Cons
186	
187	more moving parts early
188	
189	What language should you implement it in?
190	Best practical answer: C, Rust, or OCaml
191	C
192	This is the most traditional choice, and it matches part of the upstream runtime model.
193	
194	Why choose C
195	
196	simple portability story
197	
198	easy to interface with low-level runtime and GC work
199	
200	classic compiler literature maps well to it
